course_info.title: Project Management Internship
course_info.meeting_schedule: Minimum 120 hours at internship site, biweekly faculty check-ins

class_schedule.topic: Internship Orientation and Learning Plan Development
learning_objectives: Understand internship expectations, requirements, and evaluation criteria
Develop a learning plan with specific, measurable objectives
Establish communication cadence with site supervisor and faculty advisor
readings: PMI Talent Triangle: Technical, Leadership, Strategic

lecture_notes.introduction: The internship is where theory meets practice. You will apply PM skills in a real organizational context and reflect on the gap between textbook and reality.
key_concepts: Learning plan: 3-5 specific objectives aligned with PMI Talent Triangle
Time logging: minimum 120 hours documented with activities and reflection
Biweekly check-ins: faculty advisor sessions to discuss progress and challenges
Site supervisor evaluation: mid-point and final evaluations of professional performance
Reflection journal: weekly entries connecting experience to course learning
lecture_notes.demonstration: Reviewing example learning plans from past interns and discussing what makes objectives measurable

review.value: Must be specific and measurable: not 'learn about risk management' but 'facilitate at least 2 risk identification sessions and create a risk register'
Weekly reflection transforms experience into learning; connect what you observe to PM theory and identify gaps

class_schedule.topic: Professional PM Practice and Organizational Context
learning_objectives: Observe and document the organization's PM methodology
Compare the organization's practices with PMBOK and agile frameworks
Identify the organizational culture's impact on project management

lecture_notes.introduction: Every organization does PM differently. Observing how theory adapts to practice is a key learning outcome.
key_concepts: Methodology assessment: what PM framework does the organization use? How closely do they follow it?
Tailoring: how has the organization adapted standard PM processes to fit their context?
Organizational culture: how does culture affect PM practices (e.g., risk tolerance, decision speed, hierarchy)?
PM maturity: where does the organization fall on the maturity spectrum?
Gap analysis: differences between textbook PM and what actually happens
lecture_notes.demonstration: Faculty-led discussion of interns' initial observations with comparison across different organizations

review.value: Organizations rarely follow PMBOK exactly; understanding WHY they deviate is more valuable than noting the deviation
Identify where the organization is strong and where it has gaps; this is useful context for your final report recommendations

learning_objectives: Review progress against learning plan objectives
Discuss challenges encountered and strategies for overcoming them
Identify specific PM skills being applied and developed

lecture_notes.introduction: The mid-point check-in ensures you are on track to meet your learning objectives and provides an opportunity to adjust course.
key_concepts: Progress review: which learning objectives are on track, at risk, or behind?
Challenge identification: what obstacles are preventing progress?
Skill application log: which PM skills have you used? Which are missing from your experience?
Adjustment: modifying learning plan objectives if the internship context requires it
Professional development: feedback from site supervisor, areas for improvement
lecture_notes.demonstration: Structured peer discussion of internship experiences with faculty facilitation

review.value: Internship plans rarely go exactly as expected; demonstrating ability to adapt while maintaining learning objectives is a PM skill itself
If your assigned role doesn't cover a learning objective, ask to attend meetings, shadow other PMs, or take on additional tasks

learning_objectives: Identify one area where the organization's PM practices could be improved
Develop a recommendation grounded in PM theory and organizational context
Present the recommendation diplomatically and constructively

lecture_notes.introduction: Contributing a thoughtful improvement recommendation demonstrates that you can observe, analyze, and add value beyond task execution.
key_concepts: Observation: identify a specific PM practice that could be improved (not a general complaint)
Root cause: why does the current practice exist? What organizational factors sustain it?
Recommendation: specific, actionable improvement grounded in PM theory
Diplomacy: frame as building on strengths, not criticizing; propose, don't demand
Feasibility: is the recommendation realistic given the organizational culture and constraints?
lecture_notes.demonstration: Discussing examples of tactful improvement recommendations that interns have successfully made

review.value: As an intern, recommend with humility; frame as 'I noticed X and wonder if Y might help' not 'you should do Z'
The recommendation should reference specific PM concepts; this demonstrates the value of your education

learning_objectives: Synthesize internship experience into a coherent professional narrative
Present key learnings, challenges, and growth areas
Connect internship experience to future career development

lecture_notes.introduction: The final presentation synthesizes your internship into a professional development story. What did you learn, how did you grow, and where are you going?
key_concepts: Experience synthesis: organizing 120+ hours into key themes and learnings
Theory-practice connections: specific examples where coursework applied (or didn't apply) in practice
Growth narrative: where you started, challenges you faced, how you developed
Improvement recommendation: presenting your organizational improvement idea
Career direction: how the internship informed your understanding of PM career paths
lecture_notes.demonstration: Final intern presentations to faculty and peers with Q&A and feedback

review.value: The best reflections connect specific experiences to general principles; 'I learned that stakeholder management is important' is weak; 'When the manufacturing VP blocked our deployment because we hadn't consulted him, I understood why the power-interest grid matters' is strong
Honest reflection about mistakes and growth demonstrates professional maturity more than a list of accomplishments

major_assignments.title: Internship Portfolio
major_assignments.description: Complete the internship experience and produce a comprehensive portfolio including learning plan, time log, reflection journal, organizational PM assessment, improvement recommendation, and final presentation.
objectives: Complete minimum 120 hours of supervised PM-related work
Document and reflect on PM skills applied and developed
Analyze the organization's PM practices against theoretical frameworks
Develop and present a professional improvement recommendation
deliverables: Learning plan with measurable objectives (submitted week 1)
Time log documenting 120+ hours with activity descriptions
Weekly reflection journal entries (minimum 12 entries, 300+ words each)
Organizational PM assessment comparing practices to PMBOK/Agile frameworks (5-6 pages)
Final presentation (10-15 minutes) and Q&A defense
Site supervisor mid-point and final evaluations

criteria: 120+ hours documented with sufficient detail
Activities align with learning plan objectives
Time log is complete and professional
12+ substantive entries demonstrating genuine reflection
Entries connect experience to PM theory and coursework
Growth and learning are evident across the journal arc
PM practices accurately described and categorized
Comparison to PMBOK/Agile frameworks is specific and insightful
Organizational context (culture, maturity, structure) analyzed
Specific, actionable improvement identified
Root cause analysis is thorough
Recommendation grounded in PM theory and organizational reality
Presentation is clear, professional, and demonstrates synthesis
Q&A responses show deep understanding of the experience
Site supervisor evaluations are positive (mid-point and final)
Professional conduct throughout the internship
Complete hours with thorough documentation
Reflective journal demonstrating genuine learning
Insightful organizational PM assessment
Professional presentation and positive supervisor evaluations

internship_portfolio.description: Complete internship portfolio including all deliverables

site_supervisor_evaluation.description: Mid-point and final evaluations from the internship site supervisor

class_participation.description: Participation in biweekly check-ins and peer discussions

course_policies.attendance: Biweekly faculty check-ins are mandatory. Internship site attendance per site supervisor's requirements.
course_policies.late_submissions: Reflection journal entries due weekly. Final portfolio due on the last day of class. Late submissions penalized 10% per day.
course_policies.collaboration: The internship is individual. Peer discussions during check-ins are collaborative and expected.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Reflection journals must be your own honest reflection. Fabricating hours or experiences is grounds for course failure.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Word processor for journals and reports. Presentation software. Tools used at the internship site as required.
